clarification_answers.quickship_metrics: The $200K ARR was our run-rate based on the most recent quarter's revenue extrapolated. Actual trailing 12-month revenue was lower as we were growing rapidly.
clarification_answers.notion_duration: I started in mid-2020 and left in early 2022. I rounded to full years on my resume.
clarification_answers.team_size: At QuickShip, we had 2 full-time developers and 2 regular contractors who worked 30+ hours/week.

ground_truth.reasoning: Investor reference confirms ARR was projected not actual (200K claimed vs 80K real), team was 2+2 contractors not 4 FTEs, and Notion tenure was 18 months not 2 years. Multiple material misrepresentations of verifiable facts constitute fraud, even though core technical skills are genuine.

Katherine Moore, Director of Offensive Security at CrowdStrike | k.moore@crowdstrike.com
Dr. James Wilson, Professor at Virginia Tech | j.wilson@vt.edu

ref1.name: Katherine Moore
ref1.response: Nathan worked on our pen testing team. He completed around 40-50 engagements during his time here, not 100+. He is technically capable but the 'zero-day' he mentions was actually a known vulnerability that hadn't been patched. His reconnaissance framework was a useful internal tool but was used by 5 team members, not 15.

ref2.name: Dr. James Wilson
ref2.response: Nathan was a student in my cybersecurity course. He was above average but not exceptional. I'm not aware of a DEF CON speaking engagement.

clarification_answers.engagement_count: I counted all assessments including smaller vulnerability scans and phishing simulations. Pure penetration tests were around 50.
clarification_answers.zero_day: The vulnerability I found was a novel exploitation vector for a known but unpatched CVE. I consider it a zero-day because the specific attack path was previously unknown.
clarification_answers.defcon: I presented at a DEF CON village/workshop, not on the main stage. I may have overstated this on my resume.

ground_truth.reasoning: Pervasive pattern: doubled engagement count (100+ vs 40-50 actual), misrepresented known vulnerability as zero-day, inflated tool adoption (15 vs 5 users), misrepresented DEF CON village talk as main conference. Every verifiable metric on the resume was inflated. This level of systematic deception constitutes fraud.
